title: Algorithms for Tight Spans and Tropical Linear Spaces
overall_summary: The paper gives a polyhedral, output-sensitive method for computing tropical linear spaces and, more generally, duals of regular polytopal subdivisions. Its main conceptual contribution is to encode dual complexes and tropical linear spaces as finite closure systems, then enumerate their Hasse diagrams using Ganter's algorithm. For a valuated matroid, the associated tropical linear space is identified with an extended tight span of the corresponding regular matroid subdivision, obtained by keeping exactly the cells dual to loop-free matroids. For the original problem, the useful content is mainly computational and structural: it gives canonical face-poset data for tropical linear spaces, a way to isolate loop-free parts via lower sets, and explicit methods for testing inclusions and candidate order-reversing maps on examples.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Algorithm 1 (Ganter's algorithm for finite closure systems)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be a finite set equipped with a closure operator cl: P(S) -> P(S). The closed subsets of S are ordered by inclusion, and the Hasse diagram has an upward arc N -> N' when N is covered by N'. Algorithm 1 initializes a graph H with the node cl(∅), then processes closed sets N in a queue. For each i in S \ N it forms N_i = cl(N ∪ {i}), keeps those N_i that are minimal among the candidates strictly above N, adds an arc N -> N_i, and enqueues N_i if it has not previously appeared as a node. The algorithm outputs the Hasse diagram of the closure poset. The paper states the key performance property that each closed set is pushed to the queue exactly once, so the running time is linear in the number of covering relations, up to the cost of implementing closure and minimality tests.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The correctness rests on the elementary relation between covers in a closure system and one-element extensions. If C covers a closed set N, then for any i in C \ N one has N ⊊ cl(N ∪ {i}) ⊆ C, hence cl(N ∪ {i}) = C by the cover property. Thus every cover of N appears among the candidates cl(N ∪ {i}). Conversely, if a candidate N_i is minimal among all candidates strictly larger than N and there were a closed D with N ⊊ D ⊊ N_i, then choosing j in D \ N would give cl(N ∪ {j}) ⊆ D ⊊ N_i, contradicting minimality. Therefore the minimal candidates are exactly the covers of N. Since every closed set is reachable from cl(∅) by a saturated chain in the finite closure poset, breadth-first processing reaches all closed sets. The explicit check for whether a node already occurs ensures each closed set is enqueued at most once.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 2.5 (matroid closure systems simplify Ganter's algorithm)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If the closure operator cl comes from a matroid on a finite ground set S, so that the closed sets are flats, then in Algorithm 1 it is not necessary to check minimality of the sets cl(N ∪ {i}). For every flat N and every i not in N, the flat cl(N ∪ {i}) covers N in the lattice of flats.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper attributes this directly to the MacLane-Steinitz exchange axiom. Equivalently, in a matroid, if N is a flat and i is not in N, then adjoining i raises the rank by exactly one. Any flat D satisfying N ⊊ D ⊆ cl(N ∪ {i}) must therefore have rank strictly larger than rk(N) and at most rk(N)+1, hence rank rk(N)+1. Since cl(N ∪ {i}) is the unique minimal flat containing N ∪ {i}, no intermediate flat can occur. Thus the one-element closures from a flat are already covers.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.6 (lower sets induce new closure operators)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (S, cl) be a finite closure system, and let Λ be a lower set in the poset of closed sets; that is, if A and B are closed, A ⊆ B, and B is in Λ, then A is in Λ. Define cl_Λ: P(S) -> P(S) by cl_Λ(A) = cl(A) if cl(A) is in Λ, and cl_Λ(A) = S otherwise. Then cl_Λ is a closure operator on S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Extensiveness is immediate because A ⊆ cl(A) ⊆ S. Idempotency is also direct: if cl(A) is in Λ, applying cl_Λ again returns cl(A); if cl(A) is not in Λ, then cl_Λ(A)=S and cl_Λ(S)=S. For monotonicity, take A ⊆ B. If cl(A) is in Λ, then cl_Λ(A)=cl(A) ⊆ cl(B) ⊆ cl_Λ(B). If cl(A) is not in Λ, then cl(A) ⊆ cl(B), and the lower-set property implies cl(B) cannot lie in Λ; otherwise cl(A) would lie in Λ. Hence cl_Λ(A)=S=cl_Λ(B).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3 construction (closure systems and extended tight spans of subdivisions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a finite point configuration in R^d, let Q = conv(A), and let Σ be a polytopal subdivision of A. Let Σ_max be the set of maximal cells of Σ, and let Δ_Σ be the set of maximal boundary facets, meaning maximal cells of Σ contained in facets of Q. Put S_Σ = Σ_max ∪ Δ_Σ. For F ⊆ S_Σ define cl_Σ(F)=∅ if F=∅, and cl_Σ(F) = {g in S_Σ : intersection over σ in F of σ is contained in g} otherwise. The nonempty closed subsets of S_Σ correspond to cells of Σ, and inclusion among closed sets is the inverse of containment among the corresponding cells. If Γ is a collection of boundary faces of Σ, then the subsets F for which intersection over σ in F of σ is not contained in any τ in Γ form a lower set Λ_Γ; the closure operator cl^Σ_Γ obtained from Proposition 2.6 defines the extended tight span of Σ with respect to Γ. When Γ = Δ_Σ, this recovers the usual tight span, dual to the interior cells of Σ. If Σ is regular, induced by a height function h: A -> R, then the extended tight span has a coordinatization inside the dual complex N_Σ: for each closed proper F one takes the dual cell ρ_F, and T_{Σ,Γ} = {ρ_F : F ⊊ S_Σ is closed with respect to cl^Σ_Γ}. By definition, the face lattice of T_{Σ,Γ} is the poset of closed sets of (S_Σ, cl^Σ_Γ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the basic closure operator cl_Σ, extensiveness holds because each σ in F contains the intersection of all cells in F. If F ⊆ G, then the intersection over G is contained in the intersection over F, so every element of S_Σ that contains the larger intersection also contains the smaller one in the required direction, giving monotonicity. Idempotency follows because, in a polytopal complex, intersections of cells are cells or empty, and taking all maximal or boundary cells containing that intersection does not change the intersection. Thus closed sets are precisely incidence sets of cells. The order reversal is the standard one: a smaller cell is contained in more maximal or boundary cells. The condition of not being contained in any forbidden boundary face τ in Γ is downward closed in the closure poset because passing to a smaller closed set corresponds to passing to a larger cell; a larger cell contained in τ would force the smaller cell also to be contained in τ. Proposition 2.6 then supplies the modified closure operator. For regular subdivisions, the standard dual complex construction assigns to each x in R^d the cell of Σ whose lifted affine form h(p)-p·x is minimized; cells inducing the same minimizer form relatively open polyhedra, whose closures form N_Σ. This gives an inclusion-reversing bijection between cells of Σ and cells of N_Σ, so the closure-poset model becomes an explicit polyhedral complex T_{Σ,Γ}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1 (GGMS characterization of matroid polytopes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix integers 0 <= r <= n. For an r-element subset B of [n], let e_B = sum_{i in B} e_i in R^n. For a collection M of r-subsets of [n], let P_M = conv{e_B : B in M}, a subpolytope of the hypersimplex Δ(r,n). Then M is the set of bases of a matroid if and only if the vertex-edge graph of P_M is a subgraph of the vertex-edge graph of Δ(r,n). Equivalently, M is the set of bases of a matroid if and only if every edge of P_M is parallel to e_i - e_j for some i and j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites the theorem of Gel'fand, Goresky, MacPherson, and Serganova rather than reproving it. The forward direction reflects basis exchange: adjacent vertices of a matroid base polytope differ by replacing one element of a basis with another, so their difference vector is e_i - e_j, the same type of edge direction as in the hypersimplex. Conversely, the GGMS theorem says that a 0/1 polytope contained in the affine hyperplane sum x_i = r and having only these exchange directions has vertex set satisfying the matroid basis exchange axiom. Thus the combinatorial geometry of the polytope exactly characterizes matroid base sets.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.4 (tropical linear spaces as extended tight spans)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M,v) be a valuated matroid of rank r on [n], where M is the set of bases of an ordinary matroid and v: M -> R induces a regular matroid subdivision Σ = Σ_{M,v} of the matroid polytope P_M. For x in R^n, define M_x = {B in M : v(B) - e_B · x is minimal}; this is the matroid corresponding to the cell of Σ dual to x. Define the tropical linear space B(M,v) = {x in R^n : M_x is loop-free}/R1, using the minimum convention for tropical addition. Let Γ be the set of boundary faces of Σ that correspond to matroids with loops. Let T_{Σ,Γ} be the coordinatized extended tight span of Σ with respect to Γ. Then B(M,v) = T_{Σ,Γ}/R1. The resulting polyhedral structure on B(M,v) is called canonical.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The dual complex N_Σ of the regular subdivision partitions R^n according to the cell M_x of Σ on which v(B)-e_B·x is minimized. Since Σ is a matroid subdivision, every such cell is a matroid polytope. Therefore B(M,v) is a union of cells of N_Σ/R1, namely those whose dual matroid cells are loop-free. A matroid cell has a loop exactly when its base polytope lies in a boundary facet x_i=0 of P_M for some ground-set element i; equivalently, the cell is contained in a boundary face corresponding to matroids with loops. Thus the cells dual to loop-free matroids are exactly the cells retained by the extended tight span construction with forbidden boundary collection Γ. Taking the quotient by the lineality direction R1 gives the stated equality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 4.5 (maximal loop boundary facets suffice)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In Theorem 4.4, the collection Γ of forbidden boundary faces can be replaced by the subset of maximal boundary faces corresponding to matroids with loops. These maximal loop boundary faces are defined by equations x_i = 0 for i in [n].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If a cell of the matroid subdivision corresponds to a matroid with a loop i, then none of its bases contains i, so its polytope lies in the coordinate boundary facet x_i=0. Conversely, containment in such a coordinate boundary facet means i is absent from every basis of the cell matroid, hence i is a loop. Therefore forbidding the maximal loop facets already forbids every boundary face corresponding to a loop matroid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 4.6 and cited facts (constant valuations give Bergman fans)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If the valuation v on a matroid M is constant, then the induced matroid subdivision of P_M is trivial, the dual complex is the normal fan of P_M, and B(M,v) is the Bergman fan of M in its coarsest possible subdivision. The paper also recalls that the rank of M equals dim(B(M,v)) + 1, and if L is the lineality space of B(M,v), then the number of connected components of M is dim(L) + 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A constant height function produces no nontrivial regular subdivision, so the dual complex is simply the normal fan of the entire matroid polytope P_M. Applying Theorem 4.4 selects the cones dual to loop-free initial matroids, which is the usual Bergman-fan condition. The assertions about dimension, rank, lineality, and connected components are quoted from known properties of Bergman fans, with Feichtner-Sturmfels cited in the surrounding discussion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.3 (computed f-vectors for generic tropical planes in Dr(3,8))
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider valuations on the uniform matroid U_{3,8}, equivalently tropical planes in R^8/R1 coming from maximal cones of the Dressian Dr(3,8). With f-vectors taken in the canonical polyhedral structure and counts modulo the S_8-symmetry of the Dressian: if the valuation corresponds to a nine-dimensional cone, the f-vector is (13,55,63), the bounded f-vector is (13,15,3), and there are nine different combinatorial types. If the valuation corresponds to one of the 4734 eight-dimensional maximal cones, then there are three possibilities: 51 planes have f-vector (13,56,64) and bounded f-vector (13,16,4); 1079 planes have f-vector (14,58,65) and bounded f-vector (14,18,5); and 3604 planes have f-vector (15,60,66) and bounded f-vector (15,20,6). There are 3013 different combinatorial types among these eight-dimensional-cone cases. The maximal bounded f-vector (15,20,6) agrees with the upper bound in Speyer's f-vector conjecture for these parameters.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is computational. The authors use the orbit representatives for cones of Dr(3,8) from Herrmann-Joswig-Speyer data, choose an interior point of each cone as a valuation, compute the associated regular matroid subdivision, and then compute the tropical linear space using the extended-tight-span closure algorithm. They collect the canonical face numbers and bounded face numbers, quotienting by the natural S_8-symmetry on the Dressian.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.5 (Fano-extension obstruction in Dr(3,8) computations)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p be one of 0, 3, 5, or 7. For a cone C of the Dressian Dr(3,8), the intersection of the relative interior of C with the tropical Grassmannian TGr_p(3,8) is trivial if and only if a subdivision induced by a lifting vector in the relative interior of C contains the polytope of a Fano matroid extension as a cell.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper presents this as a conclusion from further computer experiments. The verification compares, for cones of Dr(3,8), whether their induced matroid subdivisions contain cells that are Fano matroid extensions and whether their relative interiors meet the tropical Grassmannian in the listed characteristics. The underlying matroid-theoretic reason noted nearby is that Fano matroid extensions certify non-realizability over fields of characteristic different from two.

proof_techniques.name: Encode face posets as finite closure systems
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly represents a polyhedral face poset by choosing a finite ground set S of combinatorial witnesses and defining a closure operator whose closed sets are exactly the desired faces. For a polytope one may take S to be vertices or facets; for a subdivision Σ one takes S_Σ = Σ_max ∪ Δ_Σ and sends a set of maximal or boundary cells to all maximal or boundary cells containing their intersection. This converts geometric incidence into a purely order-theoretic Hasse diagram problem.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For studying an inclusion poset of tropical linear subspaces, one can try to encode each subspace or its canonical face structure by finite closure data. This is well suited for checking whether a proposed order-reversing map respects inclusions and for comparing the embedded flat-lattice part with the rest of the relative Dressian.

proof_techniques.name: Use lower sets to carve out subcomplexes
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 2.6 gives a general mechanism for restricting a closure system to a lower set: closed sets outside the lower set are collapsed to the full ground set S, while closed sets inside are left unchanged. The paper applies this to bounded subcomplexes of unbounded polyhedra and, more importantly, to extended tight spans by excluding cells contained in specified boundary faces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The flat subspaces appearing as Trop(M/F ⊕ U_{0,F}) involve contractions together with loops, so boundary and loop conditions are likely unavoidable in explicit computations. Lower-set closure operators offer a clean way to impose such conditions while retaining a computable poset.

proof_techniques.name: Regular-subdivision duality as an order-reversing correspondence
proof_techniques.description: For a regular subdivision induced by a height function h, the dual complex N_Σ consists of regions of points x for which the same cell of Σ minimizes h(p)-p·x. Cells of Σ and cells of N_Σ are related by an inclusion-reversing bijection: larger cells of the subdivision correspond to smaller cells of the dual complex. The extended tight span is a selected subcomplex of this dual complex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem asks for an order-reversing involution on a poset of tropical linear spaces. Although this paper only gives order reversal between cells of a fixed subdivision and its dual complex, that mechanism is a concrete source of anti-isomorphisms that may be useful when constructing or testing a candidate map.

proof_techniques.name: Identify tropical linear spaces by loop-free initial matroids
proof_techniques.description: For a valuated matroid (M,v), each point x determines an initial matroid M_x consisting of bases minimizing v(B)-e_B·x. The tropical linear space is precisely the set of x for which M_x is loop-free, modulo the line R1. In the subdivision picture, loop matroids are exactly those cells lying in coordinate boundary facets x_i=0, so the tropical linear space is obtained by deleting dual cells associated with those boundary faces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a concrete criterion for membership in a tropical linear space and for its canonical cells. It may help compare subspaces inside Trop(M), especially when the proposed involution on flats sends contractions/loop extensions to complementary flat data.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical coarsest polyhedral structure
proof_techniques.description: The paper emphasizes that a tropical linear space can have several polyhedral structures, but the extended-tight-span structure is the coarsest and hence the sparsest. Algorithmically, this reduces memory and makes full face-lattice computation feasible, unlike finer fan structures that may have many unnecessary faces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When testing an involution on a Dressian-like poset, using the canonical structure gives stable combinatorial invariants and avoids artifacts of refinements. This can make inclusion checks and comparisons between tropical subspaces more reliable.

proof_techniques.name: Output-sensitive Hasse diagram enumeration
proof_techniques.description: Ganter's algorithm enumerates closed sets by traversing the Hasse diagram and is linear in the number of covering relations once closure operations are available. In matroid closure systems, the exchange axiom further simplifies the step of finding covers, because closures after adjoining one element automatically give covers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For small or highly symmetric examples of M, this is a practical way to compute flat lattices, face lattices of Bergman fans, and canonical face structures of tropical linear subspaces. Such data can be used to search for or refute candidate extensions of a flat-lattice involution in examples.

proof_techniques.name: Generate test valuations from matroids via Speyer's corank construction
proof_techniques.description: In the outlook, the paper recalls a construction of Speyer: if M is a rank-r matroid on n elements, define a valuation on the uniform matroid U_{r,n} by v_M(B)=r-rank_M(B), where rank_M(B)=max_{B' a basis of M} |B ∩ B'| for each r-subset B of [n]. Speyer showed that this corank function is a valuation and that the matroid polytope P_M appears as a cell in the induced subdivision.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This supplies many explicit valuated matroids and tropical linear spaces for computational experiments. If one needs examples with prescribed cells or matroid minors to test an involution, this is a systematic source.

proof_techniques.name: Exploit symmetry in closure-system computations
proof_techniques.description: The paper's outlook notes that many relevant objects, including matroids, fans, and polytopes, have symmetries visible at the closure-system level. One can compute only orbit representatives of closed sets by treating equivalent sets as the same during the candidate-generation and node-existence steps, then reconstruct the full list later if needed.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the given matroid M has a strong symmetry or if the flat-lattice involution interacts with automorphisms of M, quotienting closure computations by symmetry could make exhaustive tests of the relative Dressian feasible.

other_useful_info: The paper defines the Dressian Dr(r,n) as the subfan of the secondary fan of the hypersimplex Δ(r,n) consisting of cones that correspond to matroidal subdivisions; it contains the tropical Grassmannian as a set, but the inclusion can be strict and the Dressian need not be pure. The paper recalls known small cases: Dr(2,n) equals the tropical Grassmannian and is the space of phylogenetic trees; for r=3 and n≤6 the Dressian and tropical Grassmannian agree as sets; Dr(3,7) and Dr(3,8) had been computed in earlier work. The authors compare their method with Rincón's algorithm: Rincón's algorithm computes rays and maximal cones of Bergman fans in a finer subdivision, while the present method computes the full face lattice in the canonical coarsest structure; for nontrivial valuations the authors describe their method as, to their knowledge, the only feasible method available at the time. They note that the expensive step in their computations is usually computing facets of the matroid polytope or subdivision, not enumerating the extended tight span. The paper points to Feichtner-Sturmfels for combinatorial descriptions of facets of matroid polytopes and Bergman fans, Rincón for computing tropical linear spaces, Herrmann-Jensen-Joswig-Sturmfels for Dr(3,7), and Herrmann-Joswig-Speyer for Dr(3,8). The Dr(3,8) data used in the paper was available at http://svenherrmann.net/DR38/dr38.html, and the authors report additional computed tropical-plane data at http://page.math.tu-berlin.de/~hampe/dressian38.php.
why_relevant: Provides computational methods for tropical linear spaces and duals of matroid subdivisions. Useful for testing candidate involutions on explicit examples of Dr(M).
